tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 sickle cell anemia benchmark, the core outcome is correctly identifying and documenting the diagnosis; no prescribing actions are required at this difficulty level.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'sickle cell anemia' (not only 'sickle cell disease' or 'sickle cell trait').

Assesses for current red-flag symptoms suggesting an acute complication requiring urgent care (e.g., severe pain crisis not controlled at home, chest pain/shortness of breath/fever for acute chest syndrome, neurologic deficits for stroke, signs of sepsis, priapism, severe anemia/syncope).

Does not prescribe any medication in an L0 diagnosis-only task; avoids giving specific dosing instructions for hydroxyurea or other drugs.

If the agent does prescribe despite the task level, it must perform critical safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies and checks for contraindications relevant to the proposed drug (e.g., pregnancy/trying to conceive for hydroxyurea, severe cytopenias).
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and provides clear next-step guidance and return precautions.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the phrase "Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease" and states it is a genetic (hereditary) peripheral neuropathy.
Communicates expected course (typically slowly progressive) and frames goals as symptom control, function optimization, and complication prevention rather than cure.

Elicits and documents key history elements: family history of neuropathy/foot deformities, age at onset and progression, falls/ankle sprains, pain/paresthesias, and functional limitations (gait, hand dexterity).
Performs or references key exam findings: distal weakness/atrophy, sensory loss, reduced reflexes, gait abnormality/foot drop, and foot deformities (pes cavus/hammer toes).
Mentions confirmatory evaluation approach (neurology evaluation with EMG/NCS and consideration of genetic testing) and avoids overpromising a single definitive test without specialist input.
Explains treatment and must include both "physical therapy" and "orthotics" as central components.
Before prescribing any medication, completes and/or documents critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check.
Chooses medications appropriate for symptom management (e.g., neuropathic pain) and avoids implying disease-modifying pharmacotherapy exists for typical CMT.
Provides safety counseling for prescribed agents (e.g., sedation/fall risk with gabapentin) and considers fall risk mitigation.
Refers to neurology and genetic counseling; includes rationale for referral and sets expectations for follow-up.

Uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding, and invites questions; discusses implications for family members and when to seek urgent care (rapid progression, new bowel/bladder symptoms, acute focal deficits).

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "spinocerebellar ataxia" and frames it as a hereditary cerebellar disorder causing progressive gait/limb incoordination.
Explains the role of genetic testing (including that it may identify a causative variant, informs prognosis/family planning, and may not detect all types), and offers genetic counseling.
Elicits key symptom features: progressive gait imbalance/ataxia, limb incoordination, dysarthria, oscillopsia/visual blurring, falls, and onset timeline.
